subject: Equity Law
lesson_title: EXCEPTIONS TO THE CAMPUS OIL PRINCIPLES

questions.question: The primary exception to the standard Campus Oil test applies where:

options.A: The applicant is a company rather than an individual
options.B: The interlocutory injunction would effectively determine the entire dispute, making a full trial pointless
options.C: The respondent is a state body or government minister
options.D: The claim involves constitutional rights

questions.explanation: The primary exception to the standard Campus Oil test arises where granting or refusing the interlocutory injunction would effectively determine the entire action — i.e., where the injunction would be dispositive of the case without the need for a full trial. In such circumstances, the court is likely to require a higher standard of case from the applicant than merely a 'fair issue to be tried.'

questions.question: In B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995], the Supreme Court held that where granting or refusing the injunction would effectively determine the action, the court should apply:

options.A: The standard Campus Oil test without modification
options.B: A heightened standard, considering whether the applicant has shown a strong case — something closer to a probability of success at trial
options.C: The American Cyanamid test instead of Campus Oil
options.D: A reduced standard, requiring only that the claim is arguable

questions.explanation: In B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995], the Supreme Court held that where the grant or refusal of an interlocutory injunction would effectively determine the action (making a full trial pointless), the court should require the applicant to show something closer to a strong case — a higher standard than the ordinary 'fair issue to be tried.' This reflects the principle that if the injunction is effectively final in its impact, greater scrutiny of the merits is justified.

questions.question: Why does the court apply a higher standard when an interlocutory injunction would be dispositive of the entire dispute?

options.A: Because dispositive injunctions are automatically unconstitutional
options.B: Because the injunction will effectively grant the applicant permanent relief without a full trial, so the court must be more satisfied about the merits
options.C: Because the respondent is entitled to a jury trial on the merits
options.D: Because the Campus Oil test was designed only for non-dispositive applications

questions.explanation: The rationale for a higher standard where the injunction would be dispositive is that, in practical effect, the court is granting permanent relief without a full hearing on the merits. If granting the injunction means the applicant wins the entire dispute without a trial, it would be unjust to grant such relief on the low 'fair issue to be tried' threshold alone. The court therefore requires stronger evidence of the merits to protect the respondent from effectively losing their case at the interlocutory stage.

questions.question: Where an injunction would prevent a person from working or carrying on their trade or profession, what modified approach do Irish courts apply?

options.A: Such injunctions are automatically refused on constitutional grounds
options.B: A heightened standard is applied, recognising that restraining a person's livelihood is a drastic step requiring careful consideration
options.C: The standard Campus Oil test applies without any modification
options.D: The court refers the matter to the Labour Court before granting the injunction

questions.explanation: Where an injunction would prevent a person from earning their livelihood — working or carrying on their trade or profession — courts apply a heightened standard and treat the application with particular caution. This was recognised in Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [1987], where the court acknowledged that restraining a person from earning their living is a drastic step. The vulnerability of the individual's economic interests receives additional weight in the balance of convenience.

questions.question: Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [1987] is relevant to the exceptions to Campus Oil because it recognised that:

options.A: Mining companies cannot be restrained from operating without a full environmental hearing
options.B: Restraining a person from earning their living is a drastic step requiring careful consideration in the injunction analysis
options.C: Public utility companies are exempt from the standard Campus Oil test
options.D: The undertaking as to damages requirement is waived for employment injunctions

questions.explanation: Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [1987] recognised that restraining a person or entity from earning their living or carrying on their business is a drastic step that requires careful consideration. This principle operates as a modification to the Campus Oil analysis, effectively giving greater weight to the harm to the respondent in the balance of convenience when the injunction would interfere with the respondent's right to earn their livelihood.

questions.question: In Maha Lingam v HSE [2005] and Bergin v Galway Clinic [2008], the Irish courts addressed injunction applications in which context?

options.A: Restraint of trade in commercial franchise agreements
options.B: Employment disputes, recognising the particular vulnerability of employees and the severe consequences of injunctions
options.C: Constitutional challenges to employment legislation
options.D: Injunctions sought by trade unions against employers

questions.explanation: Maha Lingam v HSE [2005] and Bergin v Galway Clinic [2008] both involved employment injunction applications. The courts in both cases considered the particular vulnerability of employees and the severe consequences of injunctions in employment disputes — including loss of role, professional standing, and reputational harm. These cases illustrate the modified approach applied in employment contexts where the standard Campus Oil analysis is supplemented by consideration of these special factors.

questions.question: A teacher whose dismissal is alleged to be unlawful seeks an interlocutory injunction restraining the school from replacing her pending trial. The school argues the case will not reach trial for two years. Which exception to the standard Campus Oil analysis is most relevant?

options.A: The dispositive injunction exception, as the effective relief sought is reinstatement
options.B: The livelihood exception — the injunction would prevent the teacher from working and cause severe professional harm not easily compensable in damages
options.C: The public interest exception, as education is a public service
options.D: No exception applies — the standard Campus Oil test is applied without modification

questions.explanation: This scenario engages the livelihood exception. The teacher's injunction application involves her right to earn her living and maintain her professional position. Dismissal from an employment that defines professional identity and standing may cause harm — loss of role, reputational damage, skill atrophy — not easily compensable in damages. The cases of Maha Lingam v HSE [2005] and Bergin v Galway Clinic [2008] recognise the modified analysis required in such employment contexts.

questions.question: The public interest exception to the Campus Oil test is relevant where:

options.A: The applicant is a member of the public acting as a private individual
options.B: The State or a public body is involved and additional factors — including the public interest in enforcement of legislation — come into play
options.C: The respondent is a charity or non-profit organisation
options.D: The case has been reported in the media and has attracted public attention

questions.explanation: The public interest exception arises where the State or a public body is a party and factors beyond the immediate interests of the parties must be considered — including the public interest in the enforcement of legislation, even where the constitutionality of that legislation is challenged. As confirmed in Okunade v Minister for Justice [2012], the balance of convenience in such cases must weigh not just private interests but the broader public interest implications of granting or refusing the injunction.

questions.question: A respondent in an interlocutory injunction application argues that granting the injunction against him personally would, in practical terms, mean that his company — the only defendant — would cease trading and the dispute would never reach trial. This argument is most relevant to which exception?

options.A: The livelihood exception — an individual's right to work
options.B: The dispositive injunction exception — the injunction would effectively determine the entire dispute
options.C: The public interest exception — the company's closure would harm the public
options.D: No exception applies — this is purely a commercial matter governed by Campus Oil

questions.explanation: If granting an interlocutory injunction would cause the respondent's company to cease trading, effectively ending the entire dispute without a full trial, this engages the dispositive injunction exception from B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995]. Where the injunction would be dispositive — making a full trial pointless — the court applies a more stringent standard and looks for the applicant to demonstrate a strong case, not merely a fair issue to be tried.

questions.question: Which of the following cases is NOT relevant to the exceptions to the Campus Oil principles?

options.A: B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995]
options.B: Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [1987]
options.C: Maha Lingam v HSE [2005]
options.D: Boardman v Phipps [1967]

questions.explanation: Boardman v Phipps [1967] is a House of Lords case on the no-profit rule in trustee law — it concerns the liability of a fiduciary who profits from their position, not injunctions. The three cases relevant to exceptions to Campus Oil are: B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995] (dispositive injunctions), Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [1987] (livelihood/right to trade), and Maha Lingam v HSE [2005] and Bergin v Galway Clinic [2008] (employment injunctions).

questions.question: For FE-1 examination purposes, when should a candidate apply a modified Campus Oil analysis rather than the standard test?

options.A: Whenever the applicant is a commercial entity rather than an individual
options.B: Only when the applicant expressly requests a modified analysis
options.C: When the facts indicate: (1) the injunction would be dispositive; (2) it would prevent the respondent from earning a livelihood; or (3) public interest factors are engaged
options.D: When the value of the dispute exceeds €100,000

questions.explanation: In FE-1 examination answers, a candidate should flag a modified Campus Oil analysis when the facts indicate one of the recognised exceptions: (1) the interlocutory injunction would effectively determine the entire action (B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995]); (2) the injunction would prevent the respondent from earning their livelihood (Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [1987]); or (3) the case involves public interest factors such as state parties and enforcement of legislation (Okunade [2012]). These modifications must be identified and applied to the specific facts.

questions.question: The standard 'fair issue to be tried' threshold is elevated to 'a strong case' in the dispositive injunction exception because:

options.A: Courts must protect the respondent from having their case effectively decided against them without a full merits hearing
options.B: The Oireachtas has legislated for a higher standard in all injunction cases
options.C: European Union law requires a stronger merits test for interlocutory injunctions
options.D: The applicant always benefits from a dispositive injunction and therefore the threshold must be lower

questions.explanation: The elevation to 'a strong case' in dispositive injunction scenarios protects the respondent from having their case decided against them effectively at the interlocutory stage without a full merits hearing. Since granting a dispositive injunction is the practical equivalent of the applicant winning the entire case, the court must be more satisfied about the underlying merits to avoid conferring final relief under the guise of an interlocutory order.

questions.question: An applicant seeks an interlocutory injunction restraining a former business partner from using the company's trade name. The former partner argues that the injunction would force them to close their entire business as the trade name is integral to their operation. What does this argument engage?

options.A: The Campus Oil adequacy of damages criterion only
options.B: Both the dispositive injunction exception and the livelihood exception — granting the injunction could effectively terminate the business and prevent the partner from earning a livelihood
options.C: No Campus Oil exception — trade name disputes are purely commercial and no special rule applies
options.D: The public interest exception as trade name disputes affect consumers

questions.explanation: The former partner's argument engages two potential exceptions: (1) the dispositive injunction exception if the injunction would effectively determine the dispute by forcing the business to close; and (2) the livelihood exception where the injunction would prevent the respondent from carrying on their trade or profession (Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [1987]). These exceptions require the court to apply a heightened standard and give careful weight to the respondent's interest in continuing to earn a livelihood.

questions.question: In an employment injunction case, which factor identified in Maha Lingam v HSE [2005] and Bergin v Galway Clinic [2008] receives particular consideration in the balance of convenience?

options.A: The employer's ability to hire a replacement employee
options.B: The employee's particular vulnerability, loss of professional role, and reputational harm that may not be compensable in damages
options.C: The length of service of the employee
options.D: Whether the dismissal was procedurally fair under the Unfair Dismissals Act

questions.explanation: Maha Lingam v HSE [2005] and Bergin v Galway Clinic [2008] highlight the particular vulnerability of employees in injunction applications. The balance of convenience must specifically consider: the employee's loss of professional role, the reputational harm, and the fact that these consequences may not be fully compensable in damages at trial. This reflects the broader principle that the balance of convenience must be sensitive to the nature and severity of the irreversible harm to each party.

questions.question: Which statement best describes when the public interest exception to Campus Oil might tip the balance of convenience against an applicant even where they have a good arguable case?

options.A: Where the respondent is a charity and therefore immune from injunctions
options.B: Where the State's obligation to enforce statutory provisions or protect public safety would be undermined by the injunction
options.C: Where the applicant has previously lost in other courts
options.D: Where the injunction would prevent the state from collecting taxes

questions.explanation: The public interest exception operates when granting the injunction would undermine the State's ability to enforce statutory provisions or protect public safety. For example, an injunction restraining enforcement of legislation that regulates public safety, environmental protection, or public health may be refused even where the applicant has a good arguable case on the merits, because the balance of convenience must weigh the public interest in continued enforcement against the applicant's private interest in restraining that enforcement.

questions.question: A solicitor firm seeks an interlocutory injunction restraining a former partner from practising as a solicitor within the firm's geographic area. Granting the injunction would mean the former partner cannot practise their profession at all. Which exception applies?

options.A: The dispositive injunction exception
options.B: The livelihood exception — the injunction would prevent the former partner from working in their profession
options.C: The public interest exception as access to legal services is a public concern
options.D: No exception — solicitor restraint cases are not subject to modified Campus Oil analysis

questions.explanation: Where an injunction would prevent a person from practising their profession — here, as a solicitor — the livelihood exception from Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [1987] is engaged. Restraining a professional from earning their living is a drastic step requiring careful consideration. The court will apply heightened scrutiny in the balance of convenience, giving particular weight to the former partner's right to work in their chosen profession against the firm's interest in enforcing the restraint clause.

questions.question: The relationship between B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995] and the standard Campus Oil test is best described as:

options.A: B and S Ltd replaced Campus Oil as the leading authority on interlocutory injunctions
options.B: B and S Ltd is an exception to Campus Oil — it modifies the first criterion (fair issue to be tried) upwards to a stronger merits threshold where the injunction would be dispositive
options.C: B and S Ltd applies a lower threshold than Campus Oil in commercial cases
options.D: B and S Ltd is only relevant to media and publishing injunction cases

questions.explanation: B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995] is an exception to the Campus Oil framework, not a replacement. Campus Oil remains the general test. B and S Ltd modifies the first criterion — the threshold for the applicant's case — upwards from 'fair issue to be tried' to something closer to a 'strong case' or a 'probability of success at trial' specifically where the grant or refusal of the injunction would effectively determine the entire action.

questions.question: A journalist seeks an interlocutory injunction restraining a newspaper group from sacking him for publishing an article. The newspaper argues it has the contractual right to dismiss. The journalist's case entirely depends on this injunction — if refused, he will accept the dismissal. This most clearly engages which exception?

options.A: The public interest exception as journalism serves the public
options.B: The livelihood exception only
options.C: The dispositive injunction exception — refusal of the injunction would effectively determine the dispute in the newspaper's favour without a trial
options.D: The standard Campus Oil test without modification

questions.explanation: If the journalist has stated he will accept the dismissal if the injunction is refused, then refusal of the injunction would effectively determine the entire dispute without a trial. This is the classic scenario for the B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995] dispositive injunction exception: the grant or refusal of the interlocutory order would be determinative of the whole case. The court should therefore apply a heightened standard and assess whether the journalist has shown a strong case, not merely a fair issue to be tried.

questions.question: For which of the following scenarios is it most appropriate to apply the standard Campus Oil test without any modification?

options.A: A commercial tenant seeks to restrain their landlord from re-entering premises pending a rent dispute, where the tenant will continue trading regardless of the outcome
options.B: An injunction that would shut down the respondent's entire business until trial
options.C: An employment injunction that would prevent a nurse from practising in their specialty
options.D: A state-funded body seeks an injunction that would prevent a broadcaster from transmitting while constitutional validity of a regulation is determined

questions.explanation: In Option A, the tenant continues trading regardless — the injunction is not dispositive (the tenant's business is not at risk), does not prevent the respondent from earning a livelihood (the landlord can still operate), and no public interest factors are present. This is a straightforward commercial dispute amenable to the standard Campus Oil analysis without modification. Options B, C, and D each engage one or more recognised exceptions.

questions.question: In identifying whether an exception to Campus Oil applies, the key question in the dispositive exception is:

options.A: Whether the respondent has a defence that goes to the root of the applicant's case
options.B: Whether granting or refusing the injunction will make a full trial unnecessary or pointless in practical terms
options.C: Whether the parties have previously engaged in mediation
options.D: Whether the applicant has applied for the injunction within a reasonable time

questions.explanation: The dispositive exception asks: will granting or refusing the injunction effectively resolve the entire dispute so that a full trial becomes unnecessary or pointless in practical terms? If yes, the court is effectively deciding the case at the interlocutory stage and therefore must apply a higher merits threshold — as established in B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995] — to avoid conferring permanent relief without full consideration of the merits.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct statement about the exceptions to the Campus Oil principles?

options.A: Exceptions to Campus Oil are exhaustively listed in statute and cannot be expanded by the courts
options.B: Exceptions to Campus Oil are judge-made, context-sensitive modifications that require heightened scrutiny in particular circumstances
options.C: Once an exception is identified, the Campus Oil test is entirely replaced by the European Court of Human Rights framework
options.D: Exceptions to Campus Oil only apply in cases involving constitutional rights

questions.explanation: The exceptions to the Campus Oil principles are judge-made modifications developed through case law (B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995], Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [1987], Maha Lingam v HSE [2005], Okunade [2012]). They are context-sensitive and require heightened scrutiny where specific circumstances arise — dispositive effect, impact on livelihood, public interest. They are not exhaustive or statutory, and the Campus Oil framework remains the foundation which these modifications supplement.

questions.question: An FE-1 candidate is advising a client who seeks an interlocutory injunction in a case where: (a) the injunction would prevent the respondent from trading at all; and (b) the parties agree there will be no trial as the applicant will withdraw if the injunction is refused. The candidate should advise that:

options.A: The standard Campus Oil test applies and only the balance of convenience needs to be addressed
options.B: Both the dispositive injunction exception (B and S Ltd) and the livelihood exception (Bula) are engaged, requiring the applicant to demonstrate a strong case rather than merely a fair issue to be tried
options.C: Because the parties have agreed there will be no trial, the court has no jurisdiction to grant any injunction
options.D: The case should be treated as a final hearing on the merits rather than an interlocutory application

questions.explanation: This scenario engages two exceptions simultaneously: (1) the dispositive injunction exception (B and S Ltd v Irish Auto Trader [1995]) — there will be no trial, so the injunction is effectively final; and (2) the livelihood exception (Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [1987]) — the injunction would prevent the respondent from trading entirely. Both exceptions require the court to demand more than a 'fair issue to be tried': the applicant must demonstrate a strong case, closer to a probability of success at trial.
